workflow.ordering: reviewed evidence -> atomic answer claims -> derivation and exclusions -> question wording
workflow.answer_visibility: Ground truth is evaluator-only and is never included in a query prompt.

answer_claims.statement: For simple fact retrieval, basic RAG can be comparable to or better than GraphRAG because graph expansion can add noise.
GraphRAG has its clearest reported advantages on complex reasoning, contextual summarization, and creative generation.
Supplying an entire noisy textual graph can perform worse than answering from the question alone.
Graph retrieval and standalone model reasoning each correct cases missed by the other, so neither is a universally dominant answer source.

derivation.conclusion: Route simple direct lookups toward basic retrieval, but prefer graph machinery when the task actually requires interconnected synthesis.
Graph use must also be gated on evidence quality and should preserve a non-graph fallback when retrieval is noisy or unhelpful.

acceptable_variants: The non-graph branch may be described as basic RAG, vanilla RAG, or question-only/model-only answering when the distinction is kept explicit.
Complex reasoning may be described as multi-hop or interconnected contextual reasoning.

important_negatives.statement: Do not claim that adding graph context is always beneficial or that GraphRAG should replace every non-graph route.

questions.question: A production router must choose among question-only or standalone-model answering, basic RAG, and GraphRAG before generation. Derive an evidence-based decision boundary for simple facts, interconnected synthesis, and noisy graph evidence, and explain why an always-use-the-graph policy is unsupported.

answer_claims.statement: LightRAG specifies an add-only update path that processes new documents and merges their extracted nodes and edges into the existing graph.
HippoRAG argues that new knowledge can be added as graph associations instead of recomputing a summary hierarchy.
CommunityKG-RAG claims that precomputed structures permit rapid updates but does not provide a detailed maintenance algorithm or demonstrated protocol.
MedGraphRAG leaves real-time updates as future work and neither implements nor measures them.
The practical dependency-based framework motivates refreshes but does not specify deletion, provenance repair, or re-indexing behavior.

derivation.conclusion: The strongest positive evidence concerns incremental addition, not a complete mutable-index lifecycle.
A specified add-only algorithm must be distinguished from an architectural claim and from explicitly deferred work.
None of the positive add-only evidence establishes deletion, source replacement, provenance repair, or consistency under concurrent change.

acceptable_variants: LightRAG and HippoRAG may be grouped as add-oriented designs if LightRAG's explicit procedure and HippoRAG's author-level contrast are not treated as equally demonstrated.
A full lifecycle may be called CRUD, mutable maintenance, or deletion-and-repair support.

important_negatives.statement: Do not infer full update correctness, deletion support, or provenance repair from an add-only merge or a claim of rapid updates.

questions.question: Classify the corpus evidence about changing knowledge into explicit add-only behavior, architectural update claims, and unimplemented or unspecified lifecycle work. Which systems support each class, and why does none of that evidence establish a complete deletion, provenance-repair, and re-indexing protocol?

answer_claims.statement: PathRAG retained a reported advantage over LightRAG and NaiveRAG through 50 percent random edge removal, despite degradation from graph sparsification.
GraphRAG-FI's filtering recovered part of the performance lost after 30 irrelevant and incorrect paths were injected.
KAG's graph-only logical-form route is faster but can lose accuracy when extracted subject-predicate-object facts are wrong or incomplete.
KAG uses original chunks to supplement structured results when graph facts omit detail or generation context.

derivation.conclusion: Use path robustness for missing edges, filtering for injected candidates, and a text-backed hybrid route for incorrect or incomplete extracted facts.
Robustness to deletion and robustness to added noise are different interventions and cannot be inferred from one another.

acceptable_variants: Missing-edge corruption may be called sparsification or deletion noise.
Injected-path corruption may be called additive, distractor, irrelevant, or incorrect-context noise.

important_negatives.statement: Do not present any one result as proof of robustness to every graph defect or as evidence of zero degradation.

questions.question: A graph index can fail through random edge deletion, injected irrelevant or incorrect paths, or wrong and incomplete extracted triples. Match each corruption type to the strongest defense actually supported by the corpus, and explain why the three robustness claims are not interchangeable.

answer_claims.statement: G-Retriever must balance too few semantic candidates, which can miss evidence, against too many candidates, which add distracting graph content.
GraphReader reports empirical optima at five initial nodes and 2k-token chunks; more nodes add noise and both larger and smaller chunks can lose information.
CommunityKG-RAG reports non-monotonic accuracy as sentence inclusion changes at a fixed community threshold.
ROGRAG reports lower accuracy at a 64k maximum context than at 32k, attributing the decline to harder extraction of subtle information.

derivation.conclusion: Candidate count, chunk size, sentence coverage, and model context length all exhibit evidence-recall versus distraction or extraction trade-offs.
The evidence rejects a monotonic more-context-is-better rule but does not establish one universal optimum across systems.

acceptable_variants: The shared mechanism may be described as signal dilution, distraction, context noise, or extraction difficulty if the system-specific causes remain distinct.

important_negatives.statement: Do not generalize any reported candidate, chunk, threshold, or context optimum to every corpus and model.

questions.question: Use four distinct ablations to refute the rule that retrieval quality must improve whenever a system increases candidates, chunk size, sentence coverage, or context-window size. What trade-off recurs, and which reported optima must remain system-specific?

answer_claims.statement: G-Retriever's learned structural graph token and explicit textualized graph provide complementary information.
PathRAG's path-structured prompts outperform flattened prompts, supporting preservation of within-path dependencies.
KAG combines structured graph results with original chunks so missing detail and generation context can be recovered.
Community summaries can lose specific examples, quotations, and citations that matter to answer quality.

derivation.conclusion: A loss-aware prompt contract should preserve relational order or structure while retaining source text for detail and generation context.
A thematic summary alone is insufficient when the answer requires local quotations, examples, or citations.

acceptable_variants: Source text may be described as original chunks, textualized graph evidence, or citation-bearing passages when their roles are kept distinct.
Relational order may be described as path dependencies or explicit graph structure.

important_negatives.statement: Do not claim that structure alone, flattened facts alone, or high-level summaries alone preserve every form of answer evidence.

questions.question: Design a minimal evidence-organization contract that must support both relational reasoning and citation-level detail. Which ablations or limitations show that a learned structural token, a flat fact list, a path summary, or a community summary cannot safely serve as the only representation?

answer_claims.statement: Evaluation should measure graph quality, retrieval recall and relevance, and generation accuracy, faithfulness, and coverage as separate stages.
Permissive prompts can hide retrieval failure because the model can answer from parametric knowledge.
Open mode measures end-task capability with parametric knowledge, whereas reject mode requires retrieval-grounded answering or abstention.
A contamination-aware check found direct model answers that exceeded an UltraDomain ground truth, so evaluation should target domains where the base model is weak and verify the reference answer.
When high-level questions lack direct ground truth, reliance on an LLM pairwise judge creates an acknowledged evaluation-scope limitation.

derivation.conclusion: A defensible benchmark must report retrieval separately and include a grounding-required condition that exposes unsupported answers.
Reference answers and judge-based assessments need independent checks, especially when parametric knowledge or missing direct ground truth can distort conclusions.

acceptable_variants: Reject mode may be described as closed-book-with-retrieved-evidence, grounding-required, or abstention-enforced evaluation.
Stage separation may include graph construction quality as a distinct upstream stage.

important_negatives.statement: Do not treat answer accuracy under a permissive prompt as proof that retrieval succeeded.
Do not treat an LLM judge or an unverified benchmark answer as an unquestionable ground truth.

questions.question: Specify an evaluation protocol that can tell retrieval success from parametric-knowledge success. It must address pipeline-stage metrics, open versus grounding-required prompts, questionable reference answers, and LLM-judge dependence; explain the failure each safeguard prevents.

answer_claims.statement: Dependency-based and LLM-based extraction can be exposed as interchangeable construction modes to make the cost-accuracy trade-off explicit.
Surface-syntax dependency extraction can miss implicit and context-dependent relations.
Open information extraction has alignment and consistency problems, while rigorous schema-constrained extraction requires expensive expert labor.
A shared graph schema can constrain extraction and provide the vocabulary for query decomposition.
Optimizing construction or retrieval in isolation can underperform under domain shift because the two stages are interdependent.

derivation.conclusion: Use inexpensive dependency extraction for explicit relations, but escalate ambiguous or implicit cases to LLM or schema-aware extraction while budgeting expert schema work.
Construction decisions must remain aligned with the vocabulary and routes used at query time, particularly under domain shift.

acceptable_variants: The escalation design may be described as a cascade, hybrid extractor, confidence gate, or selective LLM fallback.
A shared schema may be described as vertical alignment between indexing and retrieval.

important_negatives.statement: Do not claim that dependency parsing captures implicit relations, that open extraction is automatically consistent, or that schema rigor is free.
Do not optimize the index without checking that query decomposition can address the resulting graph vocabulary.

questions.question: For a changing professional corpus with a limited construction budget, implicit relations, and domain shift, derive a staged graph-construction policy. When should dependency extraction, LLM or open extraction, and schema-constrained work be used, and what must stay aligned with query-time retrieval?

answer_claims.statement: LightRAG routes detailed queries through entity keys and abstract queries through relation or theme keys.
PolyG selects traversal behavior from which subject, predicate, or object components are missing instead of applying one policy to every graph question.
ROGRAG attempts structured decomposition first and falls back to fuzzy dual-level matching when decomposition or verification fails.
Youtu-GraphRAG decomposes complex questions into schema-valid atomic subqueries and executes entity, triple, community, and bounded path routes.

derivation.conclusion: The initial route should depend on scope, known versus missing graph components, and whether the question can be decomposed into schema-valid atomic work.
A structured route needs an explicit fuzzy fallback when decomposition or verification is unsuccessful.

acceptable_variants: Abstract queries may be called global, thematic, or relation-level queries.
Fuzzy dual-level matching may be described as semantic entity-and-relation retrieval if it remains a failure fallback.

important_negatives.statement: Do not collapse entity/detail routing, missing-triple traversal, schema decomposition, and failure fallback into one undifferentiated graph search.

questions.question: Construct a failure-aware query router for detailed entity questions, abstract thematic questions, partially specified triples, and complex decomposable questions. State the initial route for each case and the fallback when structured decomposition or verification fails.

answer_claims.statement: GraphRAG root-level reports use more than 97 percent fewer query-time context tokens than raw-text map-reduce while retaining an advantage over vector RAG in the reported setup.
The same GraphRAG study reports only small improvements of community reports over raw-text map-reduce and a root-level quality-versus-token trade-off.
LightRAG reports fewer than 100 retrieval-keyword tokens and one API call against GraphRAG in its Legal cost analysis by avoiding per-community traversal.
HippoRAG reports single-step online retrieval as 10 to 30 times cheaper and 6 to 13 times faster than IRCoT while remaining competitive.
GraphReader accepts modestly higher token use than ReadAgent in the 256k analysis in exchange for substantially stronger task scores.

derivation.conclusion: Even within one study, lower token cost does not imply an unqualified quality win.
The papers reduce online work through different mechanisms and compare against different baselines, so their ratios form separate baseline-bound claims rather than a cross-paper speed ranking.

acceptable_variants: The answer may organize the evidence by query tokens, API calls, latency, or task-quality trade-off if every number remains attached to its original baseline and setting.

important_negatives.statement: Do not rank the systems directly by the reported percentages or ratios because the studies use different tasks, baselines, models, and cost definitions.

questions.question: Reconcile the corpus's strongest online-efficiency claims without turning incomparable numbers into a league table. For GraphRAG reports, LightRAG, HippoRAG, and GraphReader, identify the mechanism, exact comparison baseline, and any quality trade-off that must travel with the cost claim.

answer_claims.statement: GraphRAG-Integration applies separate confidence thresholds to retrieved-graph and standalone-model answers before combining retained candidates.
ROGRAG reports that verifying whether context supports the query before generation outperforms checking the completed answer afterward.
CommunityKG-RAG's fact-verification contract includes supported, refuted, and not-enough-information outcomes.
Open evaluation permits parametric knowledge, while reject evaluation requires retrieval-grounded capability and abstention when evidence is insufficient.

derivation.conclusion: An open-answer policy can gate graph and parametric candidates independently and test evidence support before generation.
When the contract requires grounding, unsupported evidence must produce an explicit not-enough-information or abstention outcome rather than a parametric guess.

acceptable_variants: Not enough information may be rendered as insufficient evidence, unknown, or abstain.
Separate confidence thresholds may be described as independently gated external and intrinsic answer candidates.

important_negatives.statement: Do not silently use parametric knowledge in a retrieval-grounded or reject-mode answer when the retrieved evidence is insufficient.
Do not conflate pre-generation evidence support, candidate confidence fusion, and post-generation answer checking.

questions.question: Define two answer-source policies: one for open answering that may combine retrieved and parametric knowledge, and one for retrieval-grounded answering that must abstain when evidence is insufficient. Where should support checking and confidence gating occur, and how should fact verification represent insufficient evidence?
